coredata.dc:title: Spin dependent transport through triangular graphene quantum dot in the presence of Rashba type spin–orbit coupling
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Modarresi, M.
Kandemir, B.S.
Roknabadi, M.R.
Shahtahmasebi, N.
coredata.dc:description: Electronic, magnetic and transport properties of a triangle graphene quantum dot have been studied in the presence of Rashba type spin–orbit coupling. The non-interacting part is written in the tight-binding model, and we use the Kene–Mele–Hubbard (KMH) Hamiltonian to describe the electron–electron and the Rashba spin–orbit interaction. While the Hubbard term split the spin up and down states, the Rashba term mixed these states. The competition between these two effects produces an interesting situation in a nano-device. Our results indicate that the magnetization of zigzag edge and spin coherence are decreased due to the Rashba spin–orbit interaction in the triangle graphene quantum dot. It is shown that the reduction of spin coherence is non-negligible even in the case of weak Rashba spin–orbit interaction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Rashba spin-orbit
Green’s function

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation of T-GQD between left and right electrodes.
The variation of eigen states for different configuration of T-GQD in the presence of Rashba spin–orbit coupling.

t
C − C
).

λ
R S O
for N=5−15.
The spin coherence as a function of electron energy for different size of T-GQD.
The spin polarization of current through the T-GQD for N=7,11 and 15.

head.ce:title: Spin dependent transport through triangular graphene quantum dot in the presence of Rashba type spin–orbit coupling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electronic, magnetic and transport properties of a triangle graphene quantum dot have been studied in the presence of Rashba type spin–orbit coupling. The non-interacting part is written in the tight-binding model, and we use the Kene–Mele–Hubbard (KMH) Hamiltonian to describe the electron–electron and the Rashba spin–orbit interaction. While the Hubbard term split the spin up and down states, the Rashba term mixed these states. The competition between these two effects produces an interesting situation in a nano-device. Our results indicate that the magnetization of zigzag edge and spin coherence are decreased due to the Rashba spin–orbit interaction in the triangle graphene quantum dot. It is shown that the reduction of spin coherence is non-negligible even in the case of weak Rashba spin–orbit interaction.

ce:section.ce:para: The graphene nano-structures such as graphene nano-ribbons, carbon nano-tubes and graphene quantum dots attract much interest for both theoretical and experimental Physicists. Transport through the nano-structures is important from the technological point of view and reveals the underlying Physics. The presences of different kind of interactions in a nano-device changes the electronic, magnetic and transport properties. Here, we study the role of electron–electron interaction in the Hubbard model together with the Rashba Spin–Orbit Coupling (RSOC) in a Triangle Graphene Quantum Dot (T-GQD) between two semi-infinite leads. It was shown that the presence of on-site electron–electron interaction in such a graphene nano-structure with zigzag edge produces spin density and a net finite magnetization on the zigzag edge of the nano-structure [1–7]. The total magnetization and spin polarized current can be controlled by using an in-plane external electric field [3,8] or an incident photon beam [2]. There are some recent experimental reports for successful synthesis [9] and growth on the Ni (111) [10] and Cu (111) [11] surfaces for the T-GQD.

The role of intrinsic and extrinsic spin–orbit coupling in the graphene is first studied by Kene and Mele [12,13]. The Rashba spin–orbit interaction is related to the external electric field or the presence of substrate. There are many recent theoretical [12–18] and experimental [19,20] works on the role of Rashba term in the electronic structure of Graphene. It was shown that large RSOC up to 225meV can be induced on graphene by Ni (111) substrate which is related to the hybridization of 3d and π states of Ni and graphene [20]. Here, we study the role of RSOC on the electronic structure, magnetization and spin transport through the T-GQD between two semi-infinite leads. The T-GQD and electrodes are described in the tight-binding and wide-band approximations, respectively. All the non-interacting Hamiltonian, Hubbard and RSOC terms are solved in a self-consistent manner to obtain the electronic and magnetic properties of the nano-structure. The total Hamiltonian can be considered as the Kene–Mele–Hubbard (KMH) model [17] for a graphene quantum dot. While the Hubbard term splits the spin up and down states, the RSOC mixes them again and introduces the spin-flip process in the transport through the nano-structure.

To study the role of electron–electron interaction together with the RSOC, we consider the T-GQD between two semi-infinite structure-less electrodes as shown in Fig. 1. In this figure, N which determines the each structure uniquely shows the number of carbon atoms in each zigzag edge of T-GQD. The total Hamiltonian of the above system contains the Hamiltonian of T-GQD, electrodes and the coupling between the molecule and left/right (L/R) electrodes. The Hamiltonian of isolated T-GQD can be written as [12–17],
(1)
H
T − G Q D
=
∑
i , σ
ε
i
C
i , σ
†
C
i , σ
+
∑
< i , j > , σ
t
i , j
[
C
i , σ
†
C
j , σ
+
C
j , σ
†
C
i , σ
] + U
∑
i , σ
C
i , σ
†
C
i , σ
C
i , − σ
†
C
i , − σ
+ i
λ
R S O
∑
< i , j > , σ
C
i , σ
†
C
j , − σ
( S ×
d
i j
)
z
σ , − σ
,
where the
C
i , σ
†
(
C
i , σ
) is the electron creation (annihilation) operator for an electron with spin σ at site i. The first two terms are the on-site energy and the hopping between nearest neighbors in the unit cell of the quantum dot, respectively. The U parameter indicates the strength of the electron–electron interaction in the Hubbard model. The last term in the Eq. (1) shows the Rashba spin–orbit coupling (RSOC) [12–16]. Recent works show the intrinsic spin–orbit coupling was overestimated [21,22]. But a relatively large amount of RSOC was reported for graphene on a substrate [19,20]. The
d
i j
is a unit vector for two nearest neighbor atoms form j to i and S is the Pauli matrix. The Hubbard term can be discussed in the mean-field approach, [2,3] and configuration-interaction method [1,5,6,8]. We set on-site energy to zero, hopping integral between nearest carbon atoms
t
C − C
= 2.7
eV and the Hubbard parameter U = 1.1
t
C − C
[3,7,8]. The value of the Rashba constant
λ
R S O
is considered as a variable in all calculations. The actual range of Rashba coupling constant is around 225
meV ≃ 0.085
t
C − C
, but we let
λ
R S O
takes up to half of the hopping between nearest neighbor atoms. Here, we neglect correlation between electrons, and use the mean-field method to solve the Hubbard term in the total Hamiltonian. Due to the dependency of Hubbard Hamiltonian on the density of spins, different terms in the total Hamiltonian Eq. (1) should be solved in a self-consistent manner.

Fig. 2 shows the evaluation of the energy states of the T-GQD in the absence and presence of RSOC. Although each structure of T-GQD has many eigen-states but we only plot states around the Fermi level which is set to zero in Fig. 2. The electron–electron interaction in the Hubbard term breaks the spin degeneracy and produces an energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) states which are shown by red and blue lines, respectively. The localized state in the Fermi level splits to the N−3 states for spin up and down electrons. All up states are below the Fermi level and occupied but the down states are empty. As a result there is a ( N − 3 ) / 2 difference between the densities of up and down electrons that produces a net magnetization in the T-GQD. By increasing the RSOC constant the gap decreases and reaches to a relatively constant value. The Rashba term does not breaks the spin degeneracy in the zero state energy, but it changes the energy and the occupation of different eigen-states. Consequently it decreases the total magnetization on the zigzag edge of T-GQD. In the absence of Rashba type spin–orbit interaction the total Hamiltonian is separated into spin up and down parts. The RSOC mixes the spin up and down states in the T-GQD region. Fig. 3 shows the contribution of spin up and down in the electronic states of T-GQD around the HOMO and LUMO which are shown with H and L in the x axis of figure. In the absence of Rashba term
λ
R S O C
= 0 , we have distinct up and down states so the contribution of spin up and down in each state is exactly 0 or 100 percent. By turning on the RSOC each state includes the contribution from the up and down spins with a finite number between 0% and 100%, as shown in Fig. 3. The main effect of RSOC is in the HOMO and LUMO states that determine the transport properties of quantum dot. The new wave-functions are the mixture of the up and down states. In this situation one electron can tunnel through the T-GQD by reversing spin which is known as the spin-flip process.

The presences of zero-states energy in the Fermi level which are localized in the zigzag edges of T-GQD produce a net finite magnetization [1–6,8,17]. We define the net spin on each site as,
ρ
i
s p i n
=
n
i
↑
−
n
i
↓
for different sites of T-GQD. In the case of
λ
R S O
= 0 the total Hamiltonian and z component of Pauli matrix are commute, so the S
z is a good quantum number and
ρ
t o t
=
∑
a l l
s i t e s
ρ
i
s p i n
which shows the total net spin is an integer number [12,23,24]. By switching the RSOC [
H
T − G Q D
,
S
z
] ≠ 0 and the total spin density of T-GQD is a non-integer number which is plotted as a function of strength of RSOC in Fig. 4. According to Fig. 4, by increasing the
λ
R S O
contribution of minority electrons in the majority states increase and the total spin density decreases. In the low
λ
R S O
region the variation of
ρ
t o t
is nearly linear and negligible but by increasing the RSOC constant the total net spin decreases dramatically down to half of the non-interacting one. The left and right leads on the both side of T-GQD act as source and drain for electron in the T-GQD. The spin dependent conductance
G
σ
σ
׳
for different spin is calculated as [23,25–28],
(2)

,

T
σ

is the energy dependent transmission probability for different spin configuration

) . The trace is over the different sites of the T-GQD and
Γ
L / R
shows the coupling between T-GQD and the left/right electrodes. The
g

r / a
= 〈 σ |
g

σ

〉 is the retard/advance spin dependent Green׳s function of the T-GQD between electrodes [2,3,29–31]. The advance Green׳s function is the complex conjugate of the retarded one.
(3)
g
r / a
( ε ) =
1
( ε ± i

+
) I − H −
Σ
L

r / a
.

The
Σ
L / R
r
= ( − i / 2 )
Γ
L / R
is the retarded self-energy of left/right electrodes. Here, we use the wide-band approximation to describe the
Σ
L / R
r
. In this approximation, the real part of self-energy is neglected and imaginary part is approximated with a constant number [3,32,33]. We approved the validity of wide-band approximation for graphene nano-ribbon electrodes in low energy region [3]. The
G
σ , σ
(
G
σ , − σ
) shows the spin conserve (spin-flip) conductance through the T-GQD in the presence of RSOC. The spin-flip process is related to the mixture of spin up and down states by RSOC as shown in Fig. 3. We introduce the spin coherence as a function of electron energy S C ( ε ) to investigate the polarization of transmitted electron,
(4)
S C ( ε ) =
G
↑ ↑
+
G
↓ ↓
−
G
↑ ↓
−
G
↓ ↑
G
↑ ↑
+
G
↓ ↓
+
G
↑ ↓
+
G
↓ ↑
.

According to the definition, − 1 < S C < + 1 , the ± 1 are correspond to the pure spin conserve and spin-flip cases. In the absence of RSOC the
T
↑ ↓
=
T
↓ ↑
= 0 and the spin coherence is equal to 1. In the case of interacting system, the SC is 1 in the Fermi level, decrease linearly around the Fermi level and has a small variation in other energy intervals. In the larger structures with longer zigzag edge the RSOC becomes more important and affect the coherence very much. Although for large amount of
λ
R S O
the spin-coherence decreases dramatically but according to Fig. 5 it has also a relatively big effect even in the case of low coupling constant. These results indicate that the effect of RSOC on the transport properties of T-GQD is non-negligible even in the case of weak interaction. Due to the relatively weak coupling of spin with the electronic environment, the spin degree of freedom is an appropriate candidate for quantum information devices. The spin decoherence in T-GQD affect its application in the future spintronic and quantum information devices. The spin dependent current is calculated by using a Landauer–Büttiker like equation for different spin configuration in the presence of RSOC [23,25,29–31],
(5)
I
σ
σ
׳
( V ) =
e ℏ
∫
d ε
2 π
T
r
σ
σ
′
(
f
L
( ε ) −
f
R
( ε ) ) ,
where
f
L / R
( ε ) =
[ exp [ β ( ε −
μ
L / R
) ] + 1 ]
− 1
is the Fermi distribution function for the left and right electrodes with the chemical potential μ
L/R =ε
f ±(eV/2) and Fermi energy ε
f . We introduce the spin-polarization S P = (
I
↑ ↑
+
I
↓ ↑
−
I
↓ ↓
−
I
↑ ↓
) / (
I
↑ ↑
+
I
↓ ↑
+
I
↓ ↓
+
I
↑ ↓
) that varies between −1 and +1 for pure spin down and up spin currents. The SP which is plotted in Fig. 6 indicates the contribution of different spin on the total current through the T-GQD. The polarization comes from the difference between the electronic structure of up and down states which is related to the Hubbard term. Due to the localized magnetization in the zigzag edges the T-GQD shows a relatively large spin polarization in the low voltage region. But in other voltages the high energy states which are not affected by the electron–electron interaction contribute in the total current, as a result the SP decreases and tends to zero for all values of
λ
R S O
. The Rashba term changes the occupation of the localized states around the Fermi level and decreases the SP in lower voltage interval. The total up and down currents contain
I
↓ ↑
and
I
↑ ↓
which indicate the spin flip currents. In the absence of RSOC
I
↓ ↑
=
I
↑ ↓
= 0 and there is no spin-flip current in the T-GQD. By applying the RSOC the non-zero spin flip currents for up and down states
I
↑ ↓
=
I
↓ ↑
≠ 0 cancel each other in the numerator of SP equation and decrease the polarization of current. Actually the same probability for the up to down and down to up spin flip processes leads to small reduction in the spin polarization. In other words, the small variation of polarization with
λ
R S O
is related to the contribution of spin flip current in the total current and its cancellation in the net spin current. But spin flip processes for both up and down states accompanying each other and reduce the spin coherence in the T-GQD very much. The study of spin coherence and polarization together reveals the Physics behind the effect of RSOC on the transport properties of nano-structures.

In summary, we study the role of RSOC on the electronic, magnetic and transport properties of T-GQD between two semi-infinite electrodes. The KMH Hamiltonian for T-GQD is solved self-consistently and the effect of RSOC was studied on the eigen values, magnetization and spin-coherence of T-GQD. The strong Rashba coupling constant decreases the total magnetization drastically. Additionally, it is shown that even a weak RSOC can affect and decrease the spin-coherence in a carbon nano-structure very much.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Dr. Pawel Potasz for valuable discussions. One of the authors (B.S. Kandemir) acknowledges support for this work under TUBİTAK Grant no. 113F103.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phys. Rev. B, Condens. Matter Mater. Phys.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: M. Wimmer, Physics, vol. Ph.D., Univ.-Verl. Regensburg, Regensburg, 2009.
